name: Embalse del Valmayor – circular by El Escorial and el Bosque de la Herrería desde Colmenarejo
name_original: Embalse del Valmayor – circular por El Escorial y el Bosque de la Herrería desde Colmenarejo
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Colmenarejo traverse a varied landscape within the Community of Madrid, characterized by its proximity to the Sierra de Guadarrama mountain range. The region features diverse ecosystems including oak and pine forests, meadows, and granite formations. Key natural features for cyclists include the Valmayor Reservoir and the River Aulencia, with many routes utilizing traditional livestock trails known as Vías Pecuarias. A significant portion of the area is preserved within the Regional Park of the Middle Course of the Guadarrama River and its Surroundings. Best touring cycling routes around Colmenarejo The most popular touring cycling route is Embalse del Valmayor – circular by El Escorial and el Bosque de la Herrería desde Colmenarejo, a 26.4 miles (42.4 km) trail that takes 3 hours 9 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers views of the Valmayor Reservoir and the historic Monastery of El Escorial. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Colmenarejo – Potato House loop from Colmenarejo, a moderate 13.4 miles (21.5 km) path. This route explores the local terrain, passing through forests and open areas typical of the Colmenarejo surroundings. Local touring cyclists also love the Retamar Bridge – El Paredón and Río Chico loop from Colmenarejo, a 15.2 miles (24.5 km) trail leading through riverine woodlands and local hills, often completed in about 2 hours 6 minutes. Touring cycling around Colmenarejo is defined by the Sierra de Guadarrama foothills, the Valmayor Reservoir, and an extensive network of Vías Pecuarias. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to different ability levels. The routes in Colmenarejo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 100 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Colmenarejo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Colmenarejo?
A: There are over 15 touring cycling routes around Colmenarejo, offering a wide range of experiences for different skill levels. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Colmenarejo?
A: Colmenarejo's routes feature diverse terrain, from the foothills of the &lt;strong&gt;Sierra de Guadarrama&lt;/strong&gt; with its oak and pine forests to serene meadows and granite formations. Many routes utilize traditional &lt;strong&gt;Vías Pecuarias&lt;/strong&gt;, offering often car-free paths. You'll find options ranging from smoothly paved roads to more challenging unpaved segments and notable climbs, catering to various preferences and abilities.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Colmenarejo?
A: Yes, Colmenarejo offers routes suitable for families. While specific 'easy' routes are available, many moderate paths can be enjoyed by those looking for a less strenuous ride. The region's network of &lt;strong&gt;Vías Pecuarias&lt;/strong&gt; and dedicated bike paths, such as those integrated into the &lt;strong&gt;CiclaMadrid network&lt;/strong&gt;, often provide safer, traffic-separated options perfect for family outings.

Q: What natural features can I explore while cycling in Colmenarejo?
A: Cycling in Colmenarejo allows you to explore significant natural features such as the &lt;strong&gt;Valmayor Reservoir&lt;/strong&gt; and the &lt;strong&gt;River Aulencia&lt;/strong&gt;. The area is also rich in diverse ecosystems, including oak and pine forests, meadows, and ash groves, all set against the backdrop of the &lt;strong&gt;Sierra de Guadarrama mountain range&lt;/strong&gt;. A significant portion of the natural environment is preserved within the &lt;strong&gt;Regional Park of the Middle Course of the Guadarrama River and its Surroundings&lt;/strong&gt;.

Q: What landmarks or points of interest can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer views of notable landmarks. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e959881801/embalse-del-valmayor-circular-by-el-escorial-and-el-bosque-de-la-herreria-desde-colmenarejo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Valmayor – circular by El Escorial and el Bosque de la Herrería desde Colmenarejo&lt;/a&gt; route provides views of the historic &lt;strong&gt;Monastery of El Escorial&lt;/strong&gt;. Other nearby attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1629985" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aulencia Castle&lt;/a&gt; and various points around the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5155198" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valmayor Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes starting from Colmenarejo?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Colmenarejo are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31839194" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Colmenarejo – Potato House loop from Colmenarejo&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 21.5 km path, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31607137" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Retamar Bridge – El Paredón and Río Chico loop from Colmenarejo&lt;/a&gt;, which is 24.5 km long.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Colmenarejo?
A: The region's diverse landscapes make it enjoyable for cycling across multiple seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, ideal for exploring the oak and pine forests. Summer can be warm, but routes near the &lt;strong&gt;Valmayor Reservoir&lt;/strong&gt; or through shaded woodlands provide cooler options. Winter cycling is also possible, though conditions in the higher parts of the &lt;strong&gt;Sierra de Guadarrama&lt;/strong&gt; may require more preparation.

Q: Are there options for more challenging touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Absolutely. For experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23822178" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valmayor Hermitage – Navalagamella loop from Colmenarejo&lt;/a&gt; offer significant distances (over 50 km) and considerable elevation gains, traversing varied terrain with rewarding views.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes in Colmenarejo?
A: Colmenarejo and its surrounding areas typically offer various parking options, especially near trailheads and town centers. Many routes start directly from Colmenarejo, making it convenient to park in designated areas within the town or at the beginning of popular cycling paths. It's advisable to check specific starting points for the best parking availability.

Q: Can I reach Colmenarejo by public transport to start a cycling tour?
A: Yes, Colmenarejo is accessible by public transport, making it possible to reach the area without a car. The region is well-connected within the Community of Madrid, and local bus services often serve Colmenarejo, allowing cyclists to bring their bikes and start their tours directly from the town.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: While specific routes may vary, Colmenarejo and nearby towns like San Lorenzo de El Escorial offer various cafes, restaurants, and pubs where you can stop for refreshments. Many routes pass through or near these villages, providing convenient opportunities to refuel and relax during your ride.

Q: What are the most popular natural areas to cycle through in Colmenarejo?
A: The most popular natural areas for touring cycling include the surroundings of the &lt;strong&gt;Valmayor Reservoir&lt;/strong&gt; and the extensive network of &lt;strong&gt;Vías Pecuarias&lt;/strong&gt;. These routes often lead through the diverse ecosystems of the &lt;strong&gt;Sierra de Guadarrama foothills&lt;/strong&gt;, offering scenic rides through oak and pine forests, meadows, and riverine woodlands.
tips: Actually the one you see in the photos is the dam of the streams. Valmayor's is the one below

The Monastery of El Escorial was built by King Philip II in the 16th century to commemorate a military victory and serve as a royal pantheon for the Habsburg dynasty.
Designed by architects Juan Bautista de Toledo and Juan de Herrera, it is characterized by its sober, Renaissance style. Today it is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

From this natural observatory the monarch contemplated the development of the works of the Monastery.
Some experts assure that these granite seats, for the king and two companions, are the remains of a thousand-year-old Celtiberian altar. From here you can perceive that the Monastery has the shape of an inverted grill, in homage to San Lorenzo, who was martyred in one of them back in the year 258, since it is located on a small hill from which the Guadarrama Valley and the Sierra de Navacerrada.

waypoints.name: Royal Monastery of San Lorenzo de El Escorial
